title: HTML & CSS Fundamentals
description: The building blocks of every website. Layouts, semantics, responsiveness.

questions.q: What is the role of HTML in a web page?
options: It provides the structure and meaning of content (headings, paragraphs, links, images)
It controls how the page looks visually
It runs interactive behavior in the browser
It hosts the page on a server
questions.explanation: HTML = structure & semantics. CSS = presentation. JavaScript = behavior. Each has a distinct role.

questions.q: What does CSS stand for?
questions.explanation: 'Cascading' refers to how multiple style rules can apply to the same element, with conflicts resolved by specificity and order.

questions.q: Which is TRUE about HTML and CSS?
options: They are declarative languages — you describe what you want, the browser figures out how to render it
They are imperative languages with step-by-step logic
They are compiled to machine code
They run on the server only
questions.explanation: Declarative thinking is fundamental to writing maintainable HTML/CSS. You describe outcomes, not procedures.

questions.q: What does the browser do with HTML and CSS?
options: Parses HTML into the DOM, applies CSS to build the render tree, then paints pixels to the screen
Compiles them to a binary executable
Translates them to a different language
questions.explanation: Knowing the rendering pipeline (parse → DOM → CSSOM → render tree → layout → paint) is essential for performance work.

questions.q: Which file extension is correct for an HTML file?
questions.explanation: Modern web pages use .html. (.htm exists for historical 8.3 filename compatibility but isn't needed today.)

questions.q: What does `<!DOCTYPE html>` do at the top of a file?
options: Tells the browser to render the page in modern (standards) mode using HTML5 rules
questions.explanation: Without DOCTYPE, browsers may switch to 'quirks mode' to emulate 1990s behavior — a debugging nightmare.

questions.q: Which tag wraps the visible content of an HTML page?
questions.explanation: <head> contains metadata (title, links, scripts). <body> contains everything users actually see.

questions.q: What goes inside the `<head>` tag?
options: Title, meta tags, links to stylesheets, scripts, and other metadata
questions.explanation: Confusion alert: <head> ≠ <h1>. <head> is metadata; <h1>-<h6> are heading elements inside <body>.

questions.q: Which is the CORRECT way to set the page title shown in the browser tab?
questions.explanation: <title> is also used by search engines and bookmarks — make it descriptive and unique per page.

questions.q: What does `<meta charset='utf-8'>` do?
options: Tells the browser the file uses UTF-8 character encoding (essential for non-ASCII text)
Adds a meta description for SEO
questions.explanation: Always include this. Without it, characters like 'ا', 'é', or '中' may display as garbage.

questions.q: What is 'semantic HTML'?
options: Using HTML tags that describe the MEANING of content, not just its appearance
questions.explanation: Semantic tags help screen readers, search engines, and other developers understand your content's structure.

questions.q: Which is a SEMANTIC HTML5 element?
questions.explanation: Other semantic elements: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <aside>, <footer>, <figure>, <time>.

questions.q: When should you use `<div>` vs `<section>`?
options: <section> for meaningful content groupings; <div> only when you need a generic container with no semantic meaning
questions.explanation: Default to semantic tags. Reach for <div> only when no semantic tag fits.

questions.q: Which is the CORRECT heading hierarchy on a page?
options: Exactly one <h1>, then <h2> for major sections, <h3> for sub-sections — no skipping levels
questions.explanation: Heading order matters for accessibility and SEO. Screen reader users navigate by heading hierarchy.

questions.q: Why is semantic HTML important for accessibility?
options: Screen readers use semantic tags to navigate and convey structure to users with visual impairments
It makes the file size smaller
It looks nicer in code editors
It runs faster in the browser
questions.explanation: Semantic HTML is the foundation of accessibility. <button> vs <div onclick> is a real, life-impacting difference.

questions.q: Which tag creates a hyperlink?
questions.explanation: <a> = anchor. The href attribute holds the URL. <link> is for stylesheet/manifest references in <head>.

questions.q: How do you open a link in a NEW tab?
options: Add target='_blank' to the <a> tag (and add rel='noopener' for security)
It always opens in a new tab
questions.explanation: Always pair target='_blank' with rel='noopener noreferrer' to prevent security/performance issues with the new tab.

questions.q: What is the difference between a RELATIVE and ABSOLUTE URL?
options: Absolute starts with https://; relative is interpreted relative to the current page's location
Absolute is longer; relative is shorter
Absolute is paid; relative is free
questions.explanation: Relative URLs (`/about` or `./page.html`) are portable across domains. Absolute (https://example.com/about) lock you to that domain.

questions.q: Which is the CORRECT way to link to an email address?
questions.explanation: mailto: is a URI scheme that opens the user's email client. You can also add ?subject= and ?body= params.

questions.q: What does Contact do?
options: Scrolls to the element with id='contact' on the same page
questions.explanation: Hash links navigate to anchors within the same page. Perfect for in-page navigation menus.

questions.q: Which tag embeds an image?
questions.explanation: is a self-closing void element. src specifies the source; alt provides accessible alternative text.

questions.q: Why is the `alt` attribute on important?
options: Screen readers read it aloud, and it's shown if the image fails to load (accessibility + fallback)
It's only required by law in some countries
questions.explanation: Decorative images can have empty alt=''. Meaningful images MUST have descriptive alt text.

questions.q: Which image format is BEST for photographs?
options: JPEG or WebP (lossy compression, good for complex color)
questions.explanation: JPEG/WebP for photos. PNG for graphics with transparency. SVG for icons/logos. AVIF is the newest, even better than WebP.

questions.q: How do you make an image RESPONSIVE in CSS?
questions.explanation: max-width: 100% prevents the image from overflowing its container. height: auto preserves aspect ratio.

questions.q: Which HTML element embeds a video file?
questions.explanation: <video> with controls attribute shows native browser playback controls. Add <source> children for multiple formats.

questions.q: Which tag wraps a form?
questions.explanation: <form> with method (GET/POST) and action (URL) attributes is the foundation of every HTML form.

questions.q: Which input type shows a date picker?
questions.explanation: HTML5 added many specialized types: date, email, tel, number, color, range, file. Each provides appropriate UI.

questions.q: What does the LABEL element do in forms?
options: Associates a text label with an input field, improving accessibility and click target size
Adds a price label to a product
questions.explanation: <label for='email'>Email</label> + <input id='email'> — clicking the label focuses the input. Critical for accessibility.

questions.q: Which attribute marks an input field as REQUIRED?
questions.explanation: <input required> triggers built-in browser validation when the form is submitted with that field empty.

questions.q: What does <input type='email'> give you that <input type='text'> doesn't?
options: Built-in email format validation and mobile keyboards optimized for email input
questions.explanation: Use the most specific input type for free UX wins — better mobile keyboards, built-in validation, accessibility hints.

questions.q:
Which CSS selector targets all

elements?
questions.explanation:
Tag selector. No prefix needed. Selects every

on the page.

questions.q: Which CSS selector targets elements with class='btn'?
questions.explanation: Class selectors use a dot (.). IDs use a hash (#). Always prefer classes for styling — IDs are higher specificity and harder to override.

questions.q: Which CSS selector targets the element with id='header'?
questions.explanation: IDs use #. Each ID must be unique per page. Use sparingly for layout styling.

questions.q: What does the selector `nav > a` target?
options: <a> elements that are DIRECT children of <nav>
All <a> elements anywhere in the page
questions.explanation: > = direct child only. nav a (space) = descendant at any nesting depth. Different behaviors!

questions.q: What is CSS specificity?
options: A scoring system that determines which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element
How specific your CSS comments are
questions.explanation: Roughly: inline > ID > class > tag. Higher specificity wins. !important overrides everything (and should be avoided).

questions.q: What are the FOUR layers of the CSS box model?
questions.explanation: Every element is a box with these layers. Mastering the box model is mastering CSS layout.

questions.q: What does `box-sizing: border-box` do?
options: Makes width/height include padding and border, instead of just content
Adds a colored border to every element
questions.explanation: Most developers set * { box-sizing: border-box; } globally. Intuitive sizing — width: 200px means 200px total, not 200px + padding + border.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between margin and padding?
options: Padding is INSIDE the border (around content); margin is OUTSIDE the border (between elements)
Margin is for text, padding is for boxes
questions.explanation: Padding adds space inside; margin adds space outside. Margins collapse between adjacent block elements; padding does not.

questions.q: What does `margin: 0 auto;` do on a block element?
options: Centers it horizontally within its parent container
questions.explanation: Classic horizontal centering trick. The auto distributes remaining space equally left and right.

questions.q: What is 'margin collapse'?
options: Vertical margins between adjacent block elements combine into a single margin (the larger of the two)
questions.explanation: Surprising for beginners — two stacked elements with margin-bottom: 20px and margin-top: 30px get 30px total gap, not 50px.

questions.q: Which is a VALID way to specify a color in CSS?
questions.explanation: CSS supports many formats. Modern code often uses hsl() (intuitive for theming) or oklch() (perceptually uniform).

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between px, em, and rem?
options: px = fixed pixels; em = relative to parent's font-size; rem = relative to root font-size
Only rem works in modern browsers
px is for fonts, em for sizes
questions.explanation: Use rem for scalable, accessible typography. em for component-internal spacing. px for borders and fixed details.

questions.q: Which unit is BEST for responsive layouts?
options: Relative units like %, rem, vw, vh — they scale with viewport and user preferences
questions.explanation: Relative units make sites adapt naturally. Fixed pixels lock the design to one viewport size.

questions.q: What does `font-family: 'Inter', sans-serif;` do?
options: Tries to use Inter first; falls back to any sans-serif font if Inter isn't available
Always uses Inter no matter what
Combines Inter and sans-serif into one font
questions.explanation: Always provide a generic fallback (serif, sans-serif, monospace, cursive). Never trust that custom fonts will always load.

questions.q: What is the difference between `font-weight: bold` and `font-weight: 700`?
options: Functionally identical — 700 is the numeric value for bold
Bold is for older browsers only
questions.explanation: Font weights are 100-900. 400 = normal, 700 = bold. Variable fonts allow any value in between.

questions.q: What's the difference between `display: block` and `display: inline`?
options: Block elements take full width and stack vertically; inline elements flow horizontally like text
Block is for buttons, inline for links
questions.explanation:
,

,

are block by default. , , are inline. display can override defaults.

questions.q: What does `display: inline-block` do?
options: Elements flow inline (side by side) but accept width, height, padding, and margin like blocks
questions.explanation: Useful for buttons in a row — inline behavior with full box-model control. Largely superseded by flexbox today.

questions.q: What does `position: absolute` do?
options: Removes the element from normal flow and positions it relative to the nearest positioned ancestor
questions.explanation: Common mistake: absolute is NOT relative to the page — it's relative to the nearest ancestor with position set (or <html>).

questions.q: What does `position: sticky` do?
options: Element scrolls with the page normally until it hits a threshold, then sticks in place
Glues the element with permanent adhesive
questions.explanation: Perfect for sticky table headers and navigation bars. position: sticky + top: 0 is the magic combo.

questions.q: What is the z-index?
options: A CSS property that controls vertical stacking order of overlapping positioned elements
questions.explanation: Higher z-index = on top. Only works on positioned elements (not position: static). Manage z-index carefully to avoid 'z-index wars'.

questions.q: What does `display: flex` do to a container?
options: Makes its DIRECT children flex items, arranged in a row by default with powerful alignment options
Makes the container itself flexible in size
questions.explanation: Flexbox transforms layout. Set display: flex on a parent and all the alignment/distribution superpowers unlock.

questions.q: How do you HORIZONTALLY center items in a flex container?
questions.explanation: In a default (row) flex container, justify-content controls horizontal alignment along the main axis.

questions.q: How do you VERTICALLY center items in a flex container?
questions.explanation: align-items controls cross-axis (perpendicular) alignment. In a row flex, that's vertical.

questions.q: How do you center BOTH horizontally AND vertically?
options: display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;
questions.explanation: The classic three lines that solved the legendary 'how do I center a div' problem forever.

questions.q: What does `flex-wrap: wrap` do?
options: Allows flex items to wrap to multiple lines when they don't fit on one line
Wraps each item in a span
questions.explanation: Default is nowrap (items shrink to fit). flex-wrap: wrap is essential for responsive layouts with variable content.

questions.q: What's CSS Grid BEST suited for?
options: Two-dimensional layouts where you control both rows AND columns precisely
questions.explanation: Flexbox = 1D (row OR column). Grid = 2D (rows AND columns). They complement each other; learn both.

questions.q: How do you create a 3-column grid?
questions.explanation: 1fr = one fraction of available space. repeat(3, 1fr) = three equal columns. 1fr is the magic Grid unit.

questions.q: What does the `gap` property do in Grid (and Flexbox)?
options: Adds consistent spacing between grid/flex items without affecting outer edges
Creates a gap in the layout
Forces items to be different sizes
questions.explanation: gap replaced the old margin-based hacks. Works in Grid (since launch) and Flexbox (since 2021).

questions.q: What is a `minmax(200px, 1fr)` value in Grid?
options: Column is at least 200px wide, but grows up to 1fr (filling available space)
questions.explanation: minmax() is essential for responsive grids that need a floor width but want to grow when there's space.

questions.q: What does `repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr))` do?
options: Creates as many 250px-min columns as fit the container, expanding them to fill space — fully responsive without media queries
questions.explanation: The ultimate responsive grid one-liner. Magical for card layouts that adapt from 1 column on mobile to N columns on desktop.

questions.q: What is 'responsive design'?
options: A design approach where the layout adapts to different screen sizes from mobile to desktop
A site that responds to clicks
questions.explanation: One codebase, all screens. The standard since ~2012, made possible by CSS media queries and flexible units.

questions.q: What is a `@media` query?
options: A CSS rule that applies styles only when the screen matches conditions (e.g., min-width: 768px)
A way to play music in CSS
questions.explanation: Media queries are how CSS becomes responsive. @media (min-width: 768px) { /* tablet+ styles */ }

questions.q: What's the 'mobile-first' approach?
options: Write base styles for mobile, then progressively enhance for larger screens via min-width media queries
questions.explanation: Mobile-first encourages cleaner code and better performance. Most production sites use it.

questions.q: Why include `<meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1'>`?
options: Tells mobile browsers to use the device width instead of an arbitrary virtual width that breaks responsive design
questions.explanation: Without this meta tag, mobile browsers may render the page at ~980px wide and zoom out — breaking your responsive layout.

questions.q: What's a COMMON responsive breakpoint range?
options: ~640px (sm), ~768px (md), ~1024px (lg), ~1280px (xl) — used by Tailwind and many others
questions.explanation: Don't overthink breakpoints — design for content, not specific devices. Add a breakpoint when your layout breaks.

questions.q: What is a CSS variable (custom property)?
options: A reusable value defined with --name and accessed with var(--name)
questions.explanation: CSS variables enable runtime theming, dark mode, dynamic colors. Native, no preprocessor needed.

questions.q: How do you DEFINE a CSS variable globally?
questions.explanation: :root targets <html>. Variables defined there are accessible everywhere. Use anywhere with var(--primary).

questions.q: How do you USE a CSS variable?
questions.explanation: var() with a fallback: var(--primary, #000) uses #000 if --primary isn't defined.

questions.q: What's a STRONG use case for CSS variables?
options: Theming — switch themes by changing a few variables on a parent, the whole UI updates
questions.explanation: Dark mode = toggle a class that redefines color variables. The entire UI updates instantly. Pure CSS.

questions.q: Can CSS variables be modified by JavaScript?
options: Yes — element.style.setProperty('--primary', '#ff0000') updates the variable live
questions.explanation: CSS variables + JS = powerful runtime customization. Perfect for color pickers, theme builders, A/B tests.

questions.q: What does `:hover` do?
options: Applies styles when the user's mouse is over the element
questions.explanation: :hover is a pseudo-class. Others: :focus, :active, :visited, :disabled, :checked, :first-child, :nth-child(n).

questions.q: What's the difference between `:hover` and `:focus`?
options: :hover is for mouse pointer; :focus is for keyboard/programmatic focus (also triggered by tab/click on inputs)
questions.explanation: Critical accessibility: always style :focus for keyboard users. Better yet, use :focus-visible for the modern best practice.

questions.q: What does `::before` do?
options: Inserts a pseudo-element BEFORE the element's content (requires content property)
Runs CSS before the page loads
questions.explanation: Pseudo-elements use :: (double colon) to distinguish from pseudo-classes. Common uses: icons, decorations, clearing floats.

questions.q: What does `:nth-child(2n)` target?
options: Every other element on the page
questions.explanation: nth-child accepts formulas: 2n = even, 2n+1 = odd, 3n = every third, 5 = the 5th specifically.

questions.q: What does `:not(.active)` target?
options: Every element that does NOT have the class 'active'
questions.explanation: :not() is the negation pseudo-class. Cleaner than writing 'select all, then exclude' logic in JavaScript.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between a CSS transition and animation?
options: Transition animates from one state to another on a trigger (like :hover); animation runs through keyframes independently
questions.explanation: Transitions are simple state-to-state. Animations support complex multi-step keyframes that play on their own.

questions.q: What does `transition: all 0.3s ease;` do?
options: Animates ALL changing properties over 0.3 seconds with an ease timing function
questions.explanation: transition: all is convenient but inefficient. Prefer naming specific properties: transition: transform 0.3s ease, opacity 0.3s ease.

questions.q: Which CSS properties are MOST performant to animate?
options: transform and opacity (GPU-accelerated, don't trigger layout)
questions.explanation: Animating layout properties (width, top, etc.) triggers expensive reflow. transform/opacity are smooth at 60fps.

questions.q: What does `@keyframes` define?
options: A reusable named animation sequence with multiple states (0%, 50%, 100%)
questions.explanation: Define once with @keyframes, apply many times with animation: name duration timing iteration. Highly reusable.

questions.q: Why should you respect `prefers-reduced-motion`?
options: Some users have vestibular disorders triggered by animation — respecting the OS setting is an accessibility win
questions.explanation: Wrap big animations in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference) — disable or tone down by default.

questions.q: What is the WCAG?
options: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — the international standard for web accessibility
questions.explanation: WCAG defines A, AA, AAA conformance levels. AA is the legal/practical target for most public websites.

questions.q: Why is COLOR CONTRAST important?
options: Low contrast makes text unreadable for users with low vision, dyslexia, or in bright light
It makes the design look fancy
questions.explanation: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast for body text, 3:1 for large text. Tools like WebAIM Contrast Checker make this easy.

questions.q: What's a TRUE statement about screen readers?
options: They rely on semantic HTML, alt text, labels, and ARIA — bad HTML makes them useless

They only read text inside

tags
They aren't used by anyone today
questions.explanation: Screen readers like NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver navigate by heading, list, landmark, form. Good HTML = good screen reader UX.

questions.q: When should you use ARIA attributes?
options: When semantic HTML isn't enough — but FIRST rule of ARIA is don't use ARIA if a native element will do
On every element to be safe
questions.explanation: Common mistake: ARIA-bombing your HTML. Native <button> beats <div role='button' aria-*> every time. Use ARIA sparingly.

questions.q: What is keyboard accessibility?
options: Every interactive element must be reachable and operable using only the keyboard (Tab, Enter, Space, Arrow keys)
Adding a keyboard shortcut to every button
questions.explanation: Test by tabbing through your page. If you can't reach or use something with keyboard alone, neither can many users.

questions.q: What is a `:has()` selector?
options: A parent selector that targets elements containing matching descendants (e.g., div:has(img))
questions.explanation: :has() is the long-awaited 'parent selector'. Universally supported in modern browsers since 2023.

questions.q: What is `container queries`?
options: A way to style elements based on the size of their CONTAINER (not just the viewport)
A media query for shipping containers
questions.explanation: Game-changer for component-based design. A card component can adapt based on the sidebar/main area it's placed in.

questions.q: What is the CSS `clamp()` function?
options: Clamps a value between a min and max with a preferred value: clamp(min, preferred, max)
questions.explanation: clamp() is perfect for fluid typography: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2vw, 1.5rem) scales smoothly between sizes.

questions.q: What does `aspect-ratio: 16 / 9` do?
options: Keeps the element at a 16:9 width/height ratio regardless of size
questions.explanation: Eliminates the old padding-bottom hack for responsive video and image containers. Set width OR height, ratio handles the other.

questions.q: What is `gap` in CSS?
options: Adds space between flex/grid items without affecting outer edges (replaces margin hacks)
questions.explanation: Now works in flexbox too (since 2021). Cleaner than child margin tricks for spacing.

questions.q: What is Tailwind CSS?
options: A utility-first CSS framework where you compose styles via class names (bg-blue-500, p-4, etc.)
A type of CSS preprocessor like SASS
questions.explanation: Tailwind dominates modern web dev. Steeper initial learning curve, faster long-term development once you internalize it.

questions.q: What is a CSS preprocessor like SASS?
options: A tool that adds variables, nesting, mixins, and functions to CSS, then compiles to standard CSS
questions.explanation: SASS was huge before CSS got variables natively. Still widely used in legacy codebases and component libraries.

questions.q: What does PostCSS do?
options: A toolchain that transforms CSS with plugins (autoprefixer, nesting, modern features for older browsers)
questions.explanation: PostCSS is the foundation of many modern tools. Autoprefixer alone (adds vendor prefixes) is a near-universal use.

questions.q: What's the BENEFIT of CSS Modules?
options: Class names are scoped to the file/component, preventing global style conflicts
questions.explanation: Scoping solves the biggest CSS pain at scale — naming conflicts. Used heavily in React/Next.js projects.

questions.q: What's a SMART CSS organization strategy for a large project?
options: Component-scoped styles (CSS Modules, Tailwind, CSS-in-JS) over global stylesheets
One giant style.css with thousands of lines
questions.explanation: Locality of behavior. Keep styles close to the markup they style. Avoid the global-stylesheet anti-pattern at scale.

questions.q: Why should you AVOID `!important` in CSS?
options: It bypasses normal specificity rules and creates maintenance nightmares — fix the underlying specificity instead
It only works in old browsers
questions.explanation: !important is a code smell. If you find yourself reaching for it, your CSS architecture likely needs work.

questions.q: What's an HTML accessibility anti-pattern?
options: <div onclick='...'> as a button instead of <button>
questions.explanation: Native <button> gives keyboard focus, Enter/Space activation, screen reader announcement, AND your visual styling — free.

questions.q: What's a COMMON beginner CSS mistake?
options: Fighting specificity by stacking more selectors instead of refactoring class structure
questions.explanation: When your CSS gets gnarly, the answer is usually 'simplify and refactor', not 'add more specificity'.

questions.q: Why is INLINE STYLE generally discouraged?
options: Hard to override, can't reuse, mixes structure with presentation, and clutters HTML
questions.explanation: Inline styles override class styles (high specificity), making them hard to theme or maintain. Use class-based styling instead.

questions.q: What's the BEST way to keep improving at HTML/CSS?
options: Build real projects, read MDN docs, study other sites' CSS, follow accessibility guidelines, practice rebuilding designs
Avoid all new features for stability
questions.explanation: Real practice + great references (MDN) + curiosity about other people's code is the proven path. Build often.
